ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for cheese baked curry chicken fried rice; mapped to closest parent class 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002678). Baked and cheese-topped preparation is a local Singaporean mamak variant not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fried rice) is the closest defensible match. The cheese-baked and curry-flavoured preparation is a local variant not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared rice dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of cheese baked curry chicken fried rice weighs approximately 650 g, as commonly served at Singaporean mamak stalls and school canteens.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Fried Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo; component cross-reference: Cooked White Rice, Chicken Breast, Curry Powder, Cheddar Cheese, Whole Egg — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Database; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for cheese baked curry chicken fried rice. Nutrient values from the input record (originally derived from GrabFood nutritional disclosure at confidence 0.7) were validated against: (1) HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Chicken Fried Rice as the closest approved primary regional source; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual component ingredients — cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), roasted chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), curry powder (FDC ID 171320), cheddar cheese (FDC ID 173414), and whole egg cooked (FDC ID 173424). The per-100g macronutrient values (energy 146 kcal, protein 6.9g, fat 8.5g, carbohydrate 11.5g) are consistent with the expected profile for a mixed fried rice dish with added cheese and curry roux at the reported portion weight of 650g. Input values are retained as the best available adapted estimate. GrabFood is not cited as a primary source; it is noted only as the origin of the input record. All nutrient evidence is grounded in HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy approved sources.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — white rice and fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care. 2021;44(5):1205-1221.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese baked curry chicken fried rice. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice fried rice (GI approximately 72, high category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The addition of curry roux, cheese, and chicken modestly lowers the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI approximately 73) due to fat and protein co-ingestion, but the dish remains in the high GI category. Glycemic load per 650g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 75.0) / 100 = 54.1. Carbohydrate per 650g serving = 11.54 × 6.5 = 75.0g. Source: Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021, Table of GI values, University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 276.9 mg/100g; a full 650g serving delivers approximately 1,800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals (2,000 mg/day).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200g per meal and request reduced curry roux and cheese topping, as both are significant sodium contributors; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or tomato can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate base; a full 650g serving delivers approximately 75g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 54, which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid full-plate servings of this dish; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reducing portion to 150–200g, and increasing non-starchy vegetable content are recommended strategies to lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.31g/100g and trans fat 0.08g/100g; a full 650g serving provides approximately 15g saturated fat and 0.5g trans fat, alongside 180mg cholesterol, which collectively elevate LDL cholesterol risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced cheese and oil in preparation, and choose a smaller portion (150–200g) to keep saturated fat intake within the recommended 7% of total daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Stir-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which promote joint inflammation; dish may contain nightshade-derived ingredients (potato in curry roux) and palm oil in commercial curry roux blocks.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the pro-inflammatory frying preparation method and potential nightshade content; if consumed, request baked-only preparation with minimal oil and verify curry roux ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Baked Curry Chicken Fried Rice is a Singaporean mamak-style fusion dish consisting of pre-cooked white rice that is first stir-fried with diced chicken, curry roux, egg, onion, garlic, and vegetable oil, then transferred to a baking dish, topped with shredded or sliced cheese, and baked until the cheese is melted and golden. The result is a dish with a crispy, cheesy crust over a fragrant, mildly spiced curry-flavoured rice base. The texture combines the slight chewiness of fried rice with the richness of melted cheese. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly spiced, with the curry roux providing warmth and depth, the cheese adding creaminess and umami, and the chicken contributing protein. This dish is commonly found at Singaporean mamak stalls and school canteens. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 146 kcal, 6.9g protein, 8.5g fat (2.3g saturated), 11.5g carbohydrate, 0.8g dietary fibre, and 277mg sodium. The dish has a high glycemic index (~72) due to the white rice base, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or blood glucose levels. The sodium content is elevated, posing a concern for hypertensive individuals. The saturated fat and cholesterol content from cheese and egg warrant moderation for cardiovascular health. The dish is halal-certified in most mamak establishments and contains allergens including milk, egg, and wheat from curry roux.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo (closest approved primary regional source: Chicken Fried Rice) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific cheese-baked curry variant; values from the input record are consistent with expected macronutrient profiles for similar dishes but cannot be rated high confidence without a direct exact match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice fried rice.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and full micronutrient panel for this composite cheese-baked curry chicken fried rice preparation are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo (Chicken Fried Rice, closest approved primary source) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-level data (cooked white rice FDC 168878, roasted chicken breast FDC 171477, curry powder FDC 171320, cheddar cheese FDC 173414, whole egg cooked FDC 173424); no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish variant. GrabFood nutritional disclosure was the origin of the input record values but is not used as a primary approved source. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); the cheese and protein content may modestly reduce the effective GI in practice. 4. Glycemic load per 650g serving = 54.1, calculated as (72 × 75.0g carbohydrate) / 100. 5. The dish is classified as mamak-style Singaporean fusion; primary_cuisine tag is mamak per input. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.08g/100g as reported in the input source; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from commercial curry roux and frying oil. 7. Calcium value of 7.69mg/100g appears low for a cheese-topped dish; this may reflect the relatively small proportion of cheese per 100g of the full dish weight and should be verified against a direct HPB or USDA source if a closer match becomes available.
